3467: Culture and Process
3468: 
3469: In the last two chapters, we have seen how different forms of
3470: archaeological theory differ in their approach to questions of
3471: epistemology (the nature of knowledge claims) and questions of testing
3472: or evaluation (analogy and middlerange theory). We will now look at how
3473: theories differ in their approach to cultural forms and social change.
3474: 
3475: How do human societies work? How do human beings relate to one another?
3476: How can any human being have the power of command over a hundred, let
3477: alone a thousand or a million, others? Were social groups in the past
3478: formed through consent and cooperation, or through conflict and power?
3479: Is social change a gradual, cumulative process, or does it arise
3480: violently through the clash of ideas and groups, through conflict and
3481: contradiction? How do the day-to-day actions of ordinary individuals
3482: making pots or feeding children relate or add up to long-term,
3483: large-scale changes like the origins of agriculture or the rise of
3484: modern capitalism? These are some of the core questions within
3485: disciplines that deal with humans and the relations between them, not
3486: just archaeology but particularly sociology, cultural anthropology, and
3487: history.
3488: 
3489: All these questions can arguably be re-phrased as: what is culture, and
3490: how and why does it change? The definition of ‘culture’ was a core
3491: concern of anthropology, most obviously in the achievement of Franz Boas
3492: in making the idea of ‘culture’ the centrepiece of the fourfield
3493: approach in North America at the end of the nineteenth century.
3494: 
3495: The word ‘culture’ appears simple at first sight, but it is desperately
3496: complex and difficult to define. One group of meanings clusters around
3497: what it means to be ‘cultured’ or ‘civilized’, and are value-laden; it
3498: is an insult to call someone ‘uncultured’. This general use of the term
3499: bleeds into much of humanistic archaeology – as we have seen, the
3500: genesis of Classical archaeology depended on a notion of what it meant
3501: to be cultured, and the term ‘civilization’ and adjective ‘civilized’
3502: are also ambiguous in this respect.
3503: 
3504: Franz Boas rejected this view. For Boas, culture was not something which
3505: one person or group had and others did not. He insisted that all human
3506: groups have cultures, in the sense of systems of ideas and symbols,
3507: cultures which make sense in themselves. Boas established what
3508: anthropologists now regard as commonplace: that culture cannot be
3509: equated to or directly derived from race, and nor can it be judged on an
3510: ethnocentric yardstick. Further, ‘culture’ is not some kind of add-on to
3511: human existence, something which humans only have time for once they
3512: have satisfied their other needs such as subsistence.
3513: 
3514: Archaeologists try to talk about ancient cultures and societies, and
3515: attempt to understand how and why they existed and changed as they did.
3516: They are thus immediately locked into the sociological and
3517: anthropological questions raised above. Yet again, we cannot choose to
3518: avoid theory in this regard. ‘Atheoretical’ archaeologists, for example,
3519: will not address such questions explicitly, but they do use metaphors
3520: that imply a particular view of how societies work.
3521: 
3522: We have already seen one such metaphor in action: the aquatic view of
3523: culture, in which ripples of diffusion spread across the cultural lake,
3524: crosscutting one another. Another popular metaphor is that of society as
3525: a body. For example, we might talk about societies or social stages
3526: reaching ‘maturity’ or use other bodily metaphors, of social ‘sickness’
3527: or ‘disease’. (Supposedly descriptive and atheoretical accounts of the
3528: rise and fall of the Roman Empire are full of such metaphors.) The
3529: bodily metaphor often involves organic rhythms of ‘youth’, ‘maturity’
3530: and ‘decline’. Works of traditional history speak of ‘the birthpangs of
3531: Protestantism’. Architectural metaphors are also popular: a society
3532: ‘collapses from within’, or a particular archaeological phase or event
3533: ‘lays the foundations for’ certain subsequent changes.
3534: 
3535: The different metaphors used by ‘atheoretical’ archaeologists to
3536: characterize societies in this way are more than just words. Consciously
3537: or unconsciously, they refer back to theoretical ideas that are part of
3538: a common intellectual currency. Many relate, for example, to
3539: nineteenth-century European conceptions of history and art history.
3540: German Romantic philosophers, in particular Hegel, talked of a zeitgeist
3541: or essential spirit. Such a spirit might be characteristic of a
3542: historical period or stage of world history, or a people or race. Such a
3543: spirit might then be used to explain a particular art style or aesthetic
3544: preference; so for example Classical columns and proportions used in
3545: Greek and Roman architecture were held to be expressions of some
3546: underlying system of humanist rationality.
3547: 
3548: An underlying conception of zeitgeist profoundly influenced European art
3549: and architectural history and its applications to archaeology, for
3550: example of the Classical, medieval and Renaissance periods. Mediated by
3551: this tradition, theoretical ideas of a culture being defined and
3552: characterized by some essential or inner spirit were implicitly
3553: translated into everyday words and phrases often used by archaeologists,
3554: particularly in popular texts: ‘the spirit of the Bronze Age’, ‘the
3555: ethic of Romanitas still held sway’, ‘the inner essence of the Celtic
3556: peoples’, ‘the medieval mind’.
3557: 
3558: To repeat, all these ideas, whether implicit or explicit, and whether
3559: right or wrong, are theoretical ideas and need to be examined and
3560: criticized as such. The idea of zeitgeist in particular is morally
3561: questionable (as well as intellectually unsustainable) when applied in
3562: racial terms: it may sound innocent enough to talk of ‘the Celtic
3563: spirit’, but reference to ‘the Indo-European spirit’ would rightly be
3564: viewed with suspicion. These ideas continue nevertheless to stand in as
3565: substitutes for more developed and explicitly theorized concepts in many
3566: traditional narratives, and to be uncritically deployed in popular
3567: debates over an ‘essence’ of cultural identity which often descend into
3568: questionable stereotypes (‘typical English reserve’, ‘natural Latino
3569: exuberance’, ‘Gallic flair’).
3570: 
3571: Culture History
3572: 
3573: You will recall from chapter 2 that more traditional forms of
3574: archaeology have a normative view of culture in which a culture is
3575: defined as a set of shared ideas. These ideas are imperfectly expressed
3576: in material culture; the varied forms of artefacts imperfectly reflect
3577: different norms (figure 5.1). In this view, one tends to stress the
3578: particularity of cultures – why and how they are different from the
3579: adjacent group. Culture history can also be seen as an idealist view, in
3580: that it is ideas and norms that are seen as being important in the
3581: definition of cultural identity.
3582: 
3583: There are two problems with the culture-historical view of how human
3584: societies work. The first problem is that it tends to be mentalist. That
3585: is, it explains why a culture is the way it is primarily by reference to
3586: what people are thinking. The identification and criticism of mentalist
3587: explanation can be found time and again in archaeology and the human
3588: sciences generally. The fatal objection to a mentalist explanation is as
3589: follows: a culture decorates its houses this way because of its cultural
3590: norms. But why does it have these cultural norms? Why not a different
3591: set of norms? Again, why is this pottery decorated in this way? Because
3592: of the norms inside the potter’s head; the potter wanted it to look that
3593: way. Why did the chicken cross the road? Because of what was inside its
3594: head: it wanted to get to the other side.
3595: 
3596: Culture-historical archaeology is full of arguments that can be
3597: criticized as being mentalist. They are especially prevalent and
3598: pernicious where religion is involved, since here the author can
3599: implicitly appeal to an unspecified inner spiritual need that the reader
3600: is lulled into assuming is ‘natural’:
3601: 
3602: No system such as [the minster system], being designed to economise both
3603: in buildings and clergy [why was it designed this way? Because clerics
3604: wanted it to be this way?], could hope to meet the needs of every
3605: Christian believer [why not? What are these needs? Are these needs
3606: really self-evident if one is not a fundamentalist Christian?] And it
3607: was inevitable that, alongside these centres, a network of private
3608: chapels, or oratories, should very quickly have developed [why was it
3609: inevitable?]. (Platt 1981: 1; comments mine)
3610: 
3611: The answers to the questions in italics can only follow if the ‘need’
3612: for churches is taken as self-evident, in need of no further
3613: explanation. Why did people have certain ideas inside their heads that
3614: led them to ‘need’ more churches? Why were the old belief systems, pagan
3615: or otherwise, now deemed inadequate? And who deemed them inadequate –
3616: why did the religious élite assume that the common people ‘needed’ these
3617: ideas? Were changing ‘needs’ of this kind anything to do with
3618: contemporary socio-economic changes, and if so, what were those changes?
3619: 
3620: Figure 5.1 David Clarke’s (1976) diagram of the normative view of
3621: culture. Members of a group produce handaxes. Each has an idea of what a
3622: handaxe should look like; by observing others and communication this
3623: idea approximates to the group idea or norm. Individual handaxes are
3624: imperfect representations of this norm, being flawed, or made with more
3625: or less skill
3626: 
3627: [c05_image001.jpg]
3628: 
3629: In short, to explain an archaeological feature or historical phenomenon
3630: by reference to a untheorized need or intention in someone’s head is to
3631: explain nothing at all. Mentalist arguments of this kind can be found
3632: lurking implicitly within many traditional archaeological and historical
3633: syntheses: they can even be built into the core of their assumptions.
3634: 
3635: The second objection raised by New Archaeologists to the normative
3636: approach to culture is that culture is just treated as a rag-tag
3637: assemblage of ideas. Why do ideas fit together in a certain way? What,
3638: if anything, does the zigging versus the zagging on the pottery have to
3639: do with varying settlement and house types? Why, in short, do certain
3640: things fit together in the way they do?
3641: 
3642: The New Archaeology’s definition of culture was therefore very
3643: different. For New Archaeology, culture was a system (figure 2.5). A
3644: system was defined by David Clarke as ‘an intercommunicating network of
3645: attributes or entities forming a complex whole’ (Clarke 1978: 495). For
3646: Flannery and Marcus, systems were characterized by exchanges of matter,
3647: energy and information among their components.
3648: 
3649: This is a very different view of culture. Instead of looking for shared
3650: norms, systems thinkers looked for different elements or subsystems and
3651: studied the relations between them. Instead of looking ‘inside’ at what
3652: people thought, what was going on inside their heads, at centre stage,
3653: they often prefer to look ‘outside’, at how their cultural system was
3654: adapted, to an outside environment.
3655: 
3656: Cultural Systems: Summary
3657: 
3658: There are six aspects of thinking about cultural systems that it is
3659: important to grasp.
3660: 
3661: (1) Cultures are, at least in part, the way they are because they are
3662: adapted to an external environment, whether that is the surrounding
3663: natural environment or that of neighbouring, competing social systems.
3664: We have seen how Lewis Binford defined culture as ‘Man’s extrasomatic
3665: means of adaptation’. Much thinking on cultural systems thus tends to
3666: have close intellectual ties to Darwinian ideas of adaptation. Many
3667: thinkers have moved away from this stress on adaptation to the external
3668: environment in recent years, as we shall see.
3669: 
3670: (2) Elements of culture, in this view, are more or less observable.
3671: Obviously you cannot dig a cultural system up directly – you will never
3672: see a ‘trade’ or ‘subsistence’ subsystem in the archaeological record.
3673: You can argue that they are observable, however, in the sense that they
3674: depend on systems of energy and information flow rather than on thoughts
3675: or norms.
3676: 
3677: To clarify: you can’t see ‘norms’ inside the mind of the potter; it’s
3678: difficult to think of a way of ‘testing’ what was going on inside his or
3679: her head, particularly if he or she has been dead for several thousands
3680: of years. On the other hand, you can think of possible ways to measure
3681: the incidence and importance of ‘trade’ for a given archaeological
3682: group. You might, for example, look at the presence and proportion of
3683: non-locally made pottery from excavated contexts. Again, you can’t dig
3684: up ‘subsistence’, but you can measure and quantify the area of land
3685: suitable for arable cultivation round a site, or the meat weight and
3686: calorific value represented by the data gleaned from a faunal
3687: assemblage. So one can see how an archaeologist might begin to construct
3688: and measure a link between, in this example, subsistence economy and
3689: trade – and might then ‘test’ this hypothesized link by reference to the
3690: archaeological record.
3691: 
3692: (3) Such systems can be modelled and are amenable to being compared from
3693: culture to culture. In this way, they can lead to comparative
3694: observations and generalizations about cultural processes.
3695: 
3696: (4) Elements of cultural systems are interdependent; subsistence, trade,
3697: ritual, social subsystems are related one to another. As a result,
3698: change in one part of the system will affect the whole leading to
3699: positive or negative feedback, homeostasis or transformation. What do
3700: these terms mean?
3701: 
3702: Many systems thinkers have suggested that cultural systems can be seen
3703: in fundamentally similar terms to other kinds of system in the natural
3704: world. Natural systems in ecology, for example, tend towards a state of
3705: balance. When they are affected by some external change, such as a
3706: change in climate or the introduction of a new predator, the whole
3707: system will, after a period of fluctuation, tend to reach a new state of
3708: overall balance through modifications in the relations between the
3709: subsystems.
3710: 
3711: So, by analogy, an environmental change (say an improvement of the
3712: climate) will affect subsistence (making farming more productive). This
3713: will enable farmers to produce more agricultural surplus, and to use
3714: that surplus to engage in more trade in luxuries or prestige goods, thus
3715: changing the trade subsystem. The new prestige goods may also affect the
3716: social subsystem; accumulation of such prestige items might bolster the
3717: position of élites, increasing the social distance between élites and
3718: commoners. The changed social and trade subsystems will then of course
3719: react back on subsistence – newly powerful élites might encourage or
3720: insist on further agricultural intensification, or initiate large-scale
3721: projects like irrigation. With negative feedback, the system reaches a
3722: new balance; this tendency towards a new equilibrium is called
3723: homeostasis. On the other hand, positive feedback might occur – for
3724: example, the new flow of prestige goods produces a transformation in the
3725: social system, new forms of ranking that are unstable, which reacts back
3726: proportionally on trade and farming as new social groups demand more
3727: surplus and trade goods.
3728: 
3729: (5) Different elements of cultural systems are linked to one another and
3730: explained by function. For example, if you want to explain ritual
3731: elaboration in a particular period, it might be explained in terms of
3732: the function of religious systems in giving legitimization to social
3733: ranking (‘we are of high status because only we have access to the
3734: gods’). Again, an intensification in agricultural production (that is,
3735: producing more food) might be linked to the need to produce a surplus
3736: for prestige purposes – again one subsystem is explained by reference to
3737: its function in terms of others.
3738: 
3739: This is important because it suggests that we can look at the
3740: significance of certain practices in the archaeological record without
3741: necessarily having to make assumptions about their symbolic meaning,
3742: which for many taking this view, is untestable. In this case, we see
3743: ritual elaboration, say through the size and form of temples and
3744: religious paraphernalia, and interpret it as legitimating an élite. We
3745: don’t need to worry about what this or that religious practice ‘meant’,
3746: what the form of the temples symbolized.
3747: 
3748: (6) Archaeologists can examine the links between subsystems in terms of
3749: correlation rather than simple causes. We might observe, for example,
3750: that over time in a particular context intensification of subsistence
3751: agriculture goes hand-in-hand with population rise. Arguing about which
3752: ‘caused’ the other is pretty fruitless, being a chicken-and-egg
3753: argument. In delineating a cultural system, however, one can note the
3754: correlation between the two and build it in within a larger model of
3755: systemic change or stability.
3756: 
3757: The Idea of Process
3758: 
3759: Closely related to the idea of culture as a system is the idea of
3760: cultural process. We have seen that New Archaeology sought to replace
3761: ‘culture history’ with ‘culture process’. Since that move, the idea of
3762: process has remained central to much of archaeological thinking.
3763: 
3764: Like so many terms in archaeological thought, ‘process’ is important and
3765: habitually used, but it remains a difficult term to define. It refers to
3766: a cluster of interconnected propositions:
3767: 
3768: (a) Process is about the ‘why?’ questions – it is about explanation, not
3769: simply narrative description. Archaeologists want to ask ‘why?’ rather
3770: than merely ‘when?’ or ‘who?’. A traditional culture sequence such as
3771: that of Childe’s (figure 2.3) may be valuable in describing a sequence,
3772: but it tells us nothing about why one culture succeeded another, or why,
3773: for example, innovations like agriculture or metallurgy spread quickly
3774: or slowly. The idea of process, then, relates closely to ideas of
3775: cultural evolution.
3776: 
3777: (b) Archaeologists want to look at the underlying process rather than
3778: the ‘noise’ on top. Why pottery decoration zigs or zags is unimportant
3779: in this view; instead, what is important is to look at pottery as one
3780: artefact of trade or of craft specialization, and to chart the process
3781: by which, for example, the long-term development of market networks
3782: relates to such specialization. Particular phenomena will always vary:
3783: as with economics or sociology, what may be important is the underlying
3784: trend.
3785: 
3786: (c) Archaeologists want to look at change in the long term. Many
3787: archaeologists would argue that if you want to do anthropology, the
3788: obvious place to start is in the present. Archaeology’s great, possibly
3789: its only, contribution to the wider study of human beings had to be
3790: through its long-term perspective which cultural anthropologists,
3791: working only in the present or recent past, do not have.
3792: 
3793: In this sense, processual thinking shares many of the concerns with the
3794: rhythms of long-term history as discussed by Annales historians such as
3795: Fernand Braudel, though at the time these parallels in thinking were
3796: little noted and it fell to a later generation of theorists writing in
3797: the 1980s to point out the similarities (see chapter 11).
3798: 
3799: Example: Cultural Process around the North Sea
3800: 
3801: In his book Dark Age Economics Richard Hodges developed a processual
3802: interpretation of the rise of towns, trade and social complexity in
3803: post-Roman northwest Europe. Again, Hodges’s arguments must be seen in
3804: context. Hodges was working against a backdrop of traditional
3805: archaeological thought that had spent a great deal of time excavating
3806: early medieval trading sites and analysing documentary evidence. Much
3807: ink had been spilt trying to determine the exact date of settlements,
3808: very often in an attempt to prove that this or that settlement was ‘the
3809: first’ and that urban life spread outwards from that centre. This work,
3810: however, had done almost nothing in explaining the re-emergence of towns
3811: and trade before ad 850, had treated the evidence in a particularistic
3812: manner and in ways that were riddled with questionable mentalist and
3813: essentialist arguments (reference to innate ‘rekindling of the spirit of
3814: trade’ or ‘the trading spirit of the Frisian peoples’).
3815: 
3816: Hodges suggested that the whole of the North Sea area should be seen as
3817: a cultural system, within which social complexity, the rise of urbanism
3818: and the growth of trade were linked. Drawing on ethnographic parallels
3819: such as the Kula exchange network, he suggested that as with chiefdom
3820: societies in general, the position of the chief or king at the apex of
3821: society depended on his ability to control the production and
3822: circulation of luxury or ‘prestige’ goods. The more successful the chief
3823: was at controlling this flow, the more he could reward his followers
3824: with gifts that in turn created reciprocal obligations.
3825: 
3826: The rise of a limited number of towns in the seventh to ninth century ad
3827: could be seen as an attempt to control this production and flow; Hodges
3828: drew attention to the planned nature of these settlements, suggesting
3829: that planning reflected central control and their royal origin. He also
3830: pointed out their location: on estuarine or riverine sites suitable for
3831: long-distance trade, away from existing centres of authority.
3832: 
3833: Note that this explanation generalizes. It puts early medieval Europe
3834: within a general classification of ‘chiefdom societies’ and seeks to
3835: understand them in that light. Now ‘chiefdom societies’ can be seen in
3836: Polynesia, the Americas, the Bronze Age in the Aegean; that is, in a
3837: variety of ethnographic, ethnohistoric and prehistoric situations
3838: spanning the globe. In all these areas other scholars have drawn
3839: attention to the importance of the flow of prestige goods, the links
3840: between prestige and chiefly power, the processes leading to greater
3841: social complexity. Citing this literature, Hodges criticized traditional
3842: scholars who had emphasized the uniqueness of northwestern Europe,
3843: suggesting memorably that we should not consider the underlying
3844: processes of cultural change to be any different in this context just
3845: because a few Northern European monks took it into their heads to write
3846: down what they thought of current events.
3847: 
3848: Such an evolving social system is more or less observable in the
3849: archaeological record. We cannot directly see trade or urbanism, but we
3850: can develop archaeological indexes of trade through statistical studies
3851: of artefacts. We cannot see social ranking directly, but we can develop
3852: an index of it in terms of burial practices, the richness or paucity of
3853: grave goods.
3854: 
3855: Cultural Process: Strengths
3856: 
3857: It has been argued by some writers that all archaeologists think in
3858: terms of cultural systems and processes. We all think of societies as
3859: functioning units, and as we have seen many ‘traditional’ writers use
3860: the metaphor of bodily systems implicitly. Much thinking in these terms
3861: is implicit within explanations, even if the writer does not use the
3862: appropriate jargon or is even consciously aware of the intellectual
3863: background to what he or she is doing.
3864: 
3865: There are three strengths of thinking in terms of systems and process
3866: which are worth outlining:
3867: 
3868: (1) It avoids the problems of mentalism discussed above. Hodges tries to
3869: explain the rise of towns and trade; he does not take the easy and
3870: fallacious route of ascribing it in mentalistic fashion to some
3871: indefinable ‘Viking spirit’; it is linked to other processes within a
3872: total picture of an evolving social system.
3873: 
3874: (2) It avoids monocausal explanations, in other words explanations that
3875: try to single out one or other cause for an event. In so doing
3876: processual thinking can combine the strengths of such explanations and
3877: avoid their weaknesses.
3878: 
3879: One example is the study of the collapse of civilizations. In studying
3880: why civilizations suddenly collapse, much fruitless academic argument
3881: has been spent in arguing over single, specific causes. The eruption of
3882: Santorini and its relation to the collapse of the Minoan civilization,
3883: the ‘Classic Maya collapse’, or the convenient attribution of collapses
3884: to sudden invasions of vast hordes of barbarian peoples, are obvious
3885: examples.
3886: 
3887: Processual thinking directs our attention back to ‘why?’ questions. It
3888: points away from sudden invasions and disasters towards an understanding
3889: of why certain stresses or events were or were not critical (figure
3890: 5.2). For example:
3891: 
3892: (a) The fall of the Roman Empire. Traditional accounts are often
3893: consumed in endless detail on this or that barbarian invasion. A
3894: processual thinker asks: Why was this barbarian invasion a fatal blow
3895: rather than easily repulsed? What internal factors lessened the ability
3896: of the Empire to respond? Conversely, what systemic or structural
3897: factors underlay the barbarian invasions – population rise, territorial
3898: stress, changes in political structure?
3899: 
3900: (b) The collapse of the Minoan civilization. In the past, many looked to
3901: specific causes, such as chance invasions of barbarians or the eruption
3902: of the volcano of Santorini. In all these cases, practical arguments
3903: raged back and forth – for example, Spyridon Marinatos’s suggestion that
3904: the Santorini eruption was primarily responsible was brought into
3905: question by new dating evidence. But thinking more broadly, pointing to
3906: particular events does not help us understand the underlying process.
3907: Why should particular events like invasion or natural disaster lead to
3908: irrecoverable decline rather than rapid regeneration?
3909: 
3910: (c) The ‘Classic Maya collapse’. Many of the great monuments of the Maya
3911: civilization of Central America were suddenly abandoned, apparently
3912: almost overnight. Again, traditional explanations looked to specific
3913: historical causes such as earthquake, invasion or natural catastrophe to
3914: explain this ‘collapse’. Processual thinkers redirect attention to what
3915: they saw as more fundamental underlying factors – the relationship
3916: between population rise and agricultural productivity, for example. By
3917: modelling the Maya civilization as a system, they draw attention to
3918: relationships between changing ‘variables’. Analysis of these variables
3919: makes an apparently sudden collapse explicable as the culmination of
3920: certain long-term processes.
3921: 
3922: (3) Cultural process is a potential source of optimism for archaeology.
3923: If all aspects of a culture are functionally linked one to another, we
3924: need not be restricted in what we talk about as archaeologists. Indeed,
3925: there is no reason why we cannot infer the rest of society from the
3926: meagre archaeological record. This is what I call the fairycake argument
3927: (with apologies to Douglas Adams): if all parts of the universe are
3928: linked to one another, the argument goes, we can infer the entirety of
3929: the universe from one small piece of fairycake. Binford used this
3930: argument when he wrote: ‘granted we cannot excavate a kinship
3931: terminology or a philosophy, but we can and do excavate the material
3932: items which functioned together with these. … The formal structure of
3933: artefact assemblages together with the between-element contextual
3934: relationships should and do present a systematic and understandable
3935: picture of the total extinct cultural system’ (Binford 1964; his
3936: emphasis)
3937: 
3938: Figure 5.2 A systems model of the ‘rise of civilization’ in Mesopotamia.
3939: After Redman (1978: figure 7.7)
3940: 
3941: [c05_image002.jpg]
3942: 
3943: The Context of Cultural Systems
3944: 
3945: So far I have described processual thinking in isolation. However, it is
3946: important to place systems thinking in its context. As with so many
3947: archaeological theories, a lot of the intellectual background to systems
3948: thinking lies in associated disciplines sharing the same fundamental
3949: intellectual issues.
3950: 
3951: As we saw earlier, the links between different cultural elements or
3952: subsystems are functional ones. In functional explanation, culture is
3953: seen as comparable to an organism such as the human body or any other
3954: complex organism – there is an organic analogy, to use a common piece of
3955: jargon. Biologists explain different parts of an organism by reference
3956: to their function within the system as a whole. For example, the form of
3957: the heart – a powerful pump – is explained by reference to its function
3958: in pumping blood which takes oxygen around the body. Sociologists
3959: explain subsystems within modern social systems by reference to their
3960: functions. For example, the form of the modern state – an administrative
3961: bureaucracy – is explained by reference to its economic, social and
3962: political function(s). Processual thinkers similarly explain elements of
3963: past cultures by reference to their function; for example, religious
3964: practices and institutions are seen in terms of their function in
3965: legitimating social hierarchies, or the presence of élites is explained
3966: with reference to their function in managing complex activities such as
3967: irrigation agriculture.
3968: 
3969: Functionalism, as a theory of culture, has been defined as the
3970: proposition that cultures are like organisms, so that the part is
3971: explained in terms of its function in relation to the whole. Cohen
3972: defines it as ‘the notion that all of the institutions, beliefs and
3973: morals of a society are interrelated as a whole, so that the method of
3974: explaining the existence of any one item in the whole is to discover the
3975: law which prescribes how this system coexists with all the others’
3976: (Cohen 1968: 34). Functionalism developed in the later nineteenth
3977: century with the rise of the human sciences as a whole. Different
3978: versions were expounded by Auguste Comte, Herbert Spencer, Emile
3979: Durkheim, Radcliffe-Brown and Malinowski (note that many of these
3980: thinkers were also associated with early attempts to fashion
3981: anthropology and sociology in a positivist mould).
3982: 
3983: We can understand functionalism better if we think about its
3984: intellectual and social context. British social anthropology in
3985: particular went hand-in-hand with the training of administrators for the
3986: Empire. Thus the one very powerful implication of functional thought was
3987: that the apparently irrational, weird and wonderful customs of ‘native’
3988: peoples, such as belief in magic and witchcraft or elaborate rules
3989: surrounding exchange systems, were functionally connected to the
3990: workings of the group as a whole. Would-be administrators were taught to
3991: think about the customs of their subjects in context and not to apply a
3992: simplistic moral or evolutionary scale. Customs should not therefore be
3993: thought of as simply irrational, relics from a previous stage of social
3994: evolution, or as the perversions of ‘savage’ life.
3995: 
3996: If customs were not irrational, neither could they be satisfactorily
3997: explained as relics of previous societies. Evolutionists had often
3998: suggested that certain customs were ‘hangovers’ from previous stages of
3999: the evolution of a society. Functional thinking was synchronic – that
4000: is, it examined how different elements of society fitted together at one
4001: particular slice of time. In other words, it said ‘Never mind about
4002: historical origins. How does this or that institution or custom function
4003: here and now, at this particular moment in time?’
4004: 
4005: Processual Thinking: Drawbacks
4006: 
4007: Thinking in terms of cultural processes therefore appears to be a very
4008: strong way of thinking about cultures in the past. It avoids many
4009: problems of traditional approaches, provides convincing explanations,
4010: and allows us to develop generalizing and comparative arguments. Yet in
4011: many theoretical circles, particularly in Britain, many of the ideas
4012: outlined above have been either abandoned or at least heavily qualified.
4013: Why?
4014: 
4015: First, there is a series of well-rehearsed criticisms of functionalism.
4016: These criticisms, some argue, can equally be applied to theories of
4017: cultural process. They include the following:
4018: 
4019: (1) There is, it is argued, a flaw at the heart of functional
4020: explanation. If we explain something by reference to its function in
4021: keeping a total system going, this does not explain where it came from
4022: historically. An over-simplified example: we explain the rise of élites
4023: in state societies by reference to their managerial functions in
4024: coordinating agricultural activities. But where did these élites come
4025: from?
4026: 
4027: One possible answer is that it was a conscious decision; but it is
4028: difficult to see a cultural group sitting down round the hearth and
4029: saying ‘OK, we’re having problems coordinating large-scale irrigation
4030: agriculture, so we’ll invent an élite and believe in them as gods. They
4031: will then function in the following ways to keep our system in
4032: equilibrium …’
4033: 
4034: A more plausible answer is to link functional with adaptive
4035: explanations; that is, to say that societies that happen to have
4036: developed such élites, for example through historical accident, are
4037: better adapted and compete more effectively within their environments.
4038: In the long run therefore they tend to be ‘selected for’ in the
4039: Darwinian sense, and in the long run they are the ones we will find in
4040: the archaeological record. Processual arguments are therefore often
4041: closely related to adaptive explanations, in terms of the way they are
4042: expressed and the sort of archaeologists who put them forward. This is a
4043: point to which we will return in chapter 10.
4044: 
4045: (2) Processual arguments depend on functional linkages. But these links
4046: are always open to doubt in specific terms. There may be alternative
4047: strategies available to individuals and cultures that are not
4048: considered.
4049: 
4050: For example, one of the classic functional linkages is that made between
4051: increasingly marginal environments and agricultural intensification. As
4052: an environment becomes more difficult to live in, it is argued, human
4053: groups will elaborate and intensify their efforts to cultivate food. At
4054: Chaco Canyon in northwestern New Mexico, archaeologists have found that
4055: in the period ad 700–800 there is an upsurge in the building of
4056: ceremonial monuments, and a transition from pithouses to stone pueblos.
4057: This activity is taken as indicative of the development of a higher
4058: level of social complexity. This level of complexity often has been
4059: understood in systemic terms. The environmental record suggests that at
4060: this time the climate at Chaco was getting drier. A drier climate, it is
4061: argued, meant that if agriculture was to be successful crops had to be
4062: irrigated rather than rely on rainfall. The large-scale coordination of
4063: labour needed to run such systems required a managerial élite.
4064: 
4065: However, alternative strategies were available: why not simply abandon
4066: the area? Or adopt population control? Why adopt this form of
4067: agriculture rather than that form? Why, specifically, does the
4068: management of irrigation agriculture require an élite; can it not be run
4069: through egalitarian cooperation between groups? It has recently been
4070: argued that reorganization, rather than collapse, is the most common
4071: human response to climatic or environmental change.
4072: 
4073: It is objected, then, that alternatives are always available: that the
4074: forms of processual thinking outlined above fail to explore why this
4075: particular strategy was adopted rather than that particular strategy.
4076: Such choices between particular adaptive strategies may depend on the
4077: particularity of cultural groups, or on cultural preferences.
4078: 
4079: (3) Functionalism cannot, it is objected, explain change adequately. A
4080: functional or systemic model can explain why a system remains stable.
4081: However, why do societies become increasingly complex?
4082: 
4083: For example, Kent Flannery wrote a classic early study of the origins of
4084: agriculture in Mexico, explaining the transition from a hunting and
4085: gathering way of life as part of a wider systemic change. He treated
4086: ‘Man and the southern highlands of Mexico … as a single complex system,
4087: composed of many subsystems, which mutually caused each other’. Culture
4088: being adaptive in this view, change was seen in terms of ‘gradual change
4089: in procurement mechanisms’, regulated by seasonality. The strengths of
4090: this argument were (a) it directed attention away from the rather
4091: tedious search for the earliest example of agriculture in the form of
4092: ‘the first corncob’; (b) it thus tried to explain the origins of
4093: agriculture merely than describe its diffusion; (c) it directed
4094: attention to agriculture as part of a gradual change in culture rather
4095: than a sudden invention; and (d) the important role of a changing
4096: environment could be stressed. One weakness, however, was the assumption
4097: that ‘without external change, [the development of agriculture] might
4098: never have occurred’ (Flannery 1973a).
4099: 
4100: It can be argued, then, that models of cultural process often require an
4101: external ‘kick’ to start them off. Again, this has led to an interest on
4102: the part of many archaeologists, particularly those working on the
4103: origins of early states, to explore how change and conflict might be
4104: ‘built in’ to processual models. I will explore this interest further in
4105: chapter 9 and below.
4106: 
4107: (4) The political implications of thinking about cultural systems can be
4108: seen as objectionable. Suppose for a moment that systems thinking in its
4109: more inflexible forms were ‘true’. What would its lessons be for us
4110: today?
4111: 
4112: (a) That the nature of social stability and transformation is
4113: inflexible, composed of long-term processes beyond the consciousness or
4114: control of mere individuals, who are only pawns in the game. These
4115: long-term processes are very complex and can only be understood with
4116: lots of impenetrable jargon (mutual causal deviation amplifying
4117: processes). However, such processes are scientifically understandable.
4118: The only people, then, who really understand how society works and are
4119: therefore in a position to make judgements on how we should organize it
4120: are scientists, ‘men in white coats’. Ordinary people, it is further
4121: implied, are not qualified to make critical comments on the judgements
4122: of ‘experts’ who should therefore be left in sole charge of social
4123: management.
4124: 
4125: (b) That history is about harmony rather than conflict. If each element
4126: of culture is functionally related to the next, it is difficult to see
4127: where conflict between groups might come from. In addition, if the moral
4128: of functionalism is that all parts of the system are adapted to one
4129: another and that there is a ‘natural’ tendency towards balance or
4130: homeostasis, then by definition we live in a well-adapted system. Rather
4131: than think in terms of class conflicts and contradictions between
4132: groups, we are encouraged to treat social discord as something that can
4133: be ‘managed away’ by the white-coated experts mentioned above. One
4134: person’s systemic management problem is another’s revolution.
4135: 
4136: This is an over-simplified version of an argument developed by Jürgen
4137: Habermas and others within the ‘critical theory’ school of social
4138: philosophy, namely that systems theory is an ideology of control. In
4139: this view, systems thinking is an authoritarian political statement
4140: masquerading as neutral science. It must be opposed, in this view, on
4141: basically political grounds.
4142: 
4143: (5) Processual thinking is an attempt to understand society from the
4144: outside. Regardless of whether one is studying Neolithic Europe or
4145: Formative Oaxaca, systemic thinkers still seem to divide up their
4146: cultures into the same elements subsistence, trade, social, ritual …
4147: Right or wrong, these are general categories imposed by the
4148: archaeologist on to particular cultures. And, of course, they are (in
4149: this argument) specifically Western, bourgeois categories.
4150: 
4151: Now it may be that we should be trying to understand culture from the
4152: inside. To clarify, if we want to understand why cultures changed in the
4153: past in may be necessary to understand something of ‘their’ worldview,
4154: ‘their’ ideas of how the world works. Now their ideas may not have
4155: divided the world up into such neat little blocks, or they may have
4156: divided the world up into different blocks.
4157: 
4158: This distinction between ‘outside’ and ‘inside’ views of a culture is
4159: one that is made time and time again in different areas of the social
4160: sciences. The anthropologist Marvin Harris describes such a distinction
4161: in terms of emic (inside) and etic (outside); sociologists talk of a
4162: distinction between behaviour (what can be objectively observed) and
4163: action (what the behaviour means to the participants). The argument that
4164: it is necessary to look at ‘their’ view of ‘their’ world is one that is
4165: central to ‘interpretive’ and ‘postprocessual’ views of archaeology.
4166: 
4167: Processual Thinking Modified
4168: 
4169: The force of the criticisms raised above was felt greatly by most
4170: scholars, and has meant that more monolithic and hard-line forms of
4171: systemic and processual thought held less and less popularity in
4172: archaeology after the early 1980s. Most current versions of processual
4173: thinking are rather modified, and have developed partly in response to
4174: some of the issues raised above.
4175: 
4176: In practice, two responses can be taken:
4177: 
4178: 1 Some archaeologists claim to reject all models of culture process and
4179: have tried to adopt entirely different ways of thinking about societies
4180: (most obviously within ‘postprocessual’ approaches; see chapter 7). In
4181: this attempt they have followed the lead of sociologists, most notably
4182: Anthony Giddens. They have managed to distance themselves from thinking
4183: in terms of cultural process with varying success.
4184: 
4185: 2 Others have tried to separate modified, looser versions of processual
4186: thinking from much of the intellectual and historical ‘baggage’ of
4187: functionalism. Several propositions are involved here:
4188: 
4189: (a) We can separate functional explanation from the sins of
4190: functionalism as described above.
4191: 
4192: (b) Processual models of culture can incorporate conflict and
4193: contradiction within their parameters.
4194: 
4195: (c) If (b) holds, then change from within the cultural system can be
4196: understood and fruitfully explored in processual terms. We don’t need to
4197: rely on an external ‘kick’ as Flannery did in his early work; we might,
4198: for example, look for class or gender inequality within a culture, and
4199: view that as a source of tension that leads to transformation.
4200: Processual models can therefore, on this argument, develop
4201: understandings of very sudden transformations, and are correspondingly
4202: less dependent on ideas of adaptation to an external environment to
4203: explain where change comes from.
4204: 
4205: (d) We can look at cognitive factors (‘their’ view of ‘their’ world)
4206: within a processual model, either by modelling ‘cognition’ as a separate
4207: subsystem or by some other means. ‘Cognitive archaeology’ will be
4208: further discussed in the next chapter.
4209: 
4210: As a result, processual thinking in archaeology, particularly in North
4211: America, has moved on from a view of cultural systems as purely or
4212: primarily adaptive to an external environment, and as monolithic
4213: entities that are characterized by harmony and homeostasis. Three more
4214: contemporary trends can be distinguished.
4215: 
4216: (1) First, a move away from purely or primarily adaptive models, and an
4217: exploration of conflict. Elizabeth Brumfiel expressed this move in
4218: processual thinking well in the title of her review essay: ‘Breaking and
4219: entering the ecosystem – gender, class and faction steal the show’
4220: (1992).
4221: 
4222: In this view, culture process remains central as a way of thinking about
4223: past cultural systems. However, there are many qualifications from
4224: earlier versions. In particular, we can look at conflict and competition
4225: between different elements within the cultural whole. The dimensions of
4226: such conflict and competition include political and social faction,
4227: class, gender, and other social groups. Conflict of this kind can lead
4228: not to homeostasis but to social transformation. So instead of seeing
4229: systems as monolithic, a stress on ideas such as heterarchy and
4230: factional competition leads us towards a much more dynamic idea of
4231: systemic change – and further, one that is much less dependent on ideas
4232: of adaptation.
4233: 
4234: I will consider this work further in chapter 9, as it can also be
4235: considered as a logical outgrowth of developments in thinking about
4236: cultural evolution and early state societies. It also owes a great deal
4237: to developments in Marxist thought, and particularly a Marxist view of
4238: political economy.
4239: 
4240: (2) ‘World systems theory’ claims to unite elements of systemic and
4241: Marxist thought. We shall discuss Marxism in the next chapter, but
4242: Marxist models clearly involve many of the factors that early systems
4243: thinking in archaeology had been criticized for ignoring: class
4244: conflict, contradiction, inequality, exploitation.
4245: 
4246: World systems theory had its origins in the analysis of the origins of
4247: modern capitalism; it is associated with the work of Emmanuel
4248: Wallerstein. Wallerstein pointed out that capitalist social formations
4249: involved more than the nation-states of Western Europe. Rather, if we
4250: wanted to understand how capitalism emerged in the fifteenth, sixteenth
4251: and seventeenth centuries, we had to look at the way different societies
4252: across the globe were brought together into a single world system. Such
4253: a system had a core, in this case of the nascent capitalist states of
4254: early modern Europe, and a periphery, the other ends of the developing
4255: trade routes with Africa, Asia and the Americas. Core and periphery were
4256: related through trading networks that had social implications at each
4257: end of the link: thus capitalist development in Europe was transformed
4258: through the arrival of increased quantities of gold and of traded
4259: commodities, while ‘native’ societies changed rapidly, often developing
4260: new élites and nascent states, partly in response to the influx of
4261: European goods. The entire globe, taken as a whole, could be modelled
4262: systemically, with stress on the interdependence between core and
4263: periphery, so that change in one could be seen to lead to change in the
4264: other, often many thousands of miles away. The social systems of both
4265: were understood by reference to functional linkages.
4266: 
4267: Archaeologists have suggested that we can use a world systems model to
4268: explain a variety of ancient social networks. In particular, they have
4269: looked at the interaction and networks of dependence between core state
4270: societies and peripheral pre-state societies in a variety of contexts
4271: (Bronze and Iron Age Europe, pre-Hispanic Central America, for example).
4272: Santley and Alexander, for example, develop a generalizing typology of
4273: units (the ‘dendritic political economy’, the ‘hegemonic empire’, the
4274: ‘territorial empire’) and then a set of processes involved in
4275: transformation (disenfranchisement, population redistribution, labour
4276: organization and power).
4277: 
4278: (3) Simulation and mathematical modelling: from the late 1970s onwards
4279: Renfrew and others argued that systems could be simulated on computers,
4280: and that moreover we could build chance, historical contingency and
4281: human decision-making into these models. This led in turn to an interest
4282: in new models of the natural world being produced by natural scientists.
4283: For example, chaos theory looks at how apparently random and small-scale
4284: phenomena can trigger much larger patterns. By analogy, many systemic
4285: thinkers have explored stochastic models (that is, models with elements
4286: of chance or random variation built into them) and asked how systems
4287: might be set off on different trajectories by contingent or chance
4288: events.
4289: 
4290: These strands of thought are very different one from another, but what
4291: is certain is that over the last 30 years there has been a ‘softening’
4292: of processual thinking. Cultural systems are no longer seen as
4293: monolithic structures, and contingency and historical accident is
4294: allowed for; room has been made for cognition (see next chapter); and
4295: heterarchy and conflict have become popular buzz-words.
4296: 
4297: However, I want to stress that an idea of process remains central and
4298: even foundational to much academic archaeology, particularly in North
4299: America; in later chapters, I shall argue that much of ‘postprocessual’
4300: archaeology is much more dependent on concepts of culture process than
4301: many of its proponents care to admit.
4302: 
4303: Culture, Process and the Individual
4304: 
4305: Stop – you’ve finished before discussing the most important criticism of
4306: processual thinking of all. Human beings don’t act like this. They’re
4307: not just pawns in a system. They are irrational and unpredictable.
4308: Processual theory can never work because human actions are random. You
4309: can’t fit human beings with all their idiosyncrasies and peculiarities
4310: into a model.
4311: 
4312: Ironically, the one criticism of processual thinking that is most
4313: popular is also the one that, in my view, is utterly misconceived.
4314: 
4315: Are human beings unpredictable? Almost certainly not, though we like to
4316: think so. Cupid is supposed to be blind, but the vast majority of us
4317: manage by some amazing coincidence to find partners of very similar
4318: ethnic and social origins to ourselves.
4319: 
4320: In any case, unless you are a methodological individualist (see
4321: Glossary), you cannot reduce the understanding of long-term social
4322: processes to what we do as individuals. This is a classic debate not
4323: just within archaeology, but within the human sciences as a whole.
4324: 
4325: Consider an example from the modern world, one examined by Emile
4326: Durkheim in a classic pioneering study in the social sciences: the
4327: suicide rate. The reasons why individuals commit suicide are as varied
4328: as they are tragic, but the overall rate of suicides within society as a
4329: whole rises and falls with other factors (such as the unemployment rate,
4330: whether the country is at peace or at war). A social scientist cannot
4331: predict individual suicides, but can, given sufficient data on
4332: contemporary social trends, predict rises and falls in the suicide rate,
4333: more often than not with a great deal of success. Consider also powerful
4334: individuals. Scholars may seek to explain events in terms of powerful or
4335: charismatic people – emperors or great leaders – but this always begs
4336: the question of where their power comes from, what makes their power
4337: possible.
4338: 
4339: If such a view works for the present, it is surely even stronger in
4340: prehistory, where archaeologists manifestly have to deal with trends and
4341: processes that span hundreds and even thousands of years, rhythms of
4342: time than are beyond the compass of any individual.
4343: 
4344: Western thought has an emotional attachment to the idea of ‘the
4345: individual’ that is simply not borne out by the reality of the world
4346: around us, and still less is a valid basis for thinking about the past.
4347: We can’t say in advance whether individuals were or were not important
4348: in the past. Other cultures have had different ideas about individuality
4349: and the importance of the individual versus the community. Westerners
4350: may find some of these ideas strange, even disturbing or objectionable,
4351: but this is no excuse for taking the modern Western ‘cult of the
4352: individual’ as self-evident, or true for all times and all places.
4353: 
4354: There is a more sophisticated argument within much of contemporary
4355: archaeology, namely that we have to understand social agency and the
4356: individual subject. Pots are made by people, and I believe that the
4357: archaeological record is as much about the detritus of individual
4358: actions as it is about long-term aggregates or processes. There is also
4359: a moral or philosophical argument that ‘freedom’ is a meaningful term to
4360: use in historical analysis, but only in the presence of the absence of
4361: freedom, for example in discussions of slavery. But before we can
4362: consider such ideas, the Romantic notion of unqualified individual
4363: freedom has to be firmly squashed. It may sell Hollywood blockbusters
4364: (count the number of mentions the word ‘freedom’ gets in Braveheart) but
4365: it is no basis for a serious analysis of past societies.
4366: 
4367: 6
4368: 
4369: Thoughts and Ideologies
4370: 
4371: The last chapter mentioned in passing the question of how we might
4372: understand or account for the thoughts of people in the past. Looking at
4373: thoughts in the minds of people long dead is obviously very difficult.
4374: Is it necessary?
4375: 
4376: The question ‘do we have to look at thoughts in the past?’ is
4377: deceptively simple. It is embedded in deeper questions, for example:
4378: 
4379: 1 What are ‘thoughts’, anyway? Are these conscious or unconscious? The
4380: major influence of Carl Jung and Sigmund Freud on the human sciences was
4381: to show that our conscious thoughts were only the tip of the iceberg;
4382: human mental processes were much deeper, more complex and more difficult
4383: to understand than the ‘rational’ surface. Should we take a ‘deep’ view
4384: of thoughts or a ‘shallow’ view?
4385: 
4386: 2 Are all human beings united by the same cognitive systems, or are
4387: these diverse? To use some common jargon, are we justified in making
4388: essentialist claims about the nature of cognition, or is human knowledge
4389: of the world socially constructed and therefore variable between
4390: different societies? If it is variable, how can we be justified in
4391: making assumptions about the individual or group psychology of
4392: prehistoric cultures on the basis of studies of modern populations?
4393: 
4394: These are all very deep questions that are not exclusive to archaeology.
4395: They are questions that all ‘human sciences’ have had to grapple with in
4396: different forms.
4397: 
4398: In this chapter, I want to expand on this theme and look at some of the
4399: ways theory in the human sciences at large has coped with this problem.
4400: These themes are, first, the influence of the structuralist and semiotic
4401: traditions of thought, second, the influence of Marxism, and third, the
4402: move towards cognitive approaches.
4403: 
4404: Looking at Thoughts
4405: 
4406: Do we really have to look at cognition, at thoughts? Many would say
4407: ‘No’. A large number of positivists both within archaeology and within
4408: the human sciences in general argue that you can never test thoughts.
4409: They give two reasons:
4410: 
4411: 1 You can never scientifically verify what is between someone’s ears; in
4412: archaeology, we can never go back to talk to the long-dead people who
4413: created the monuments and artefacts that we study. Thoughts are
4414: therefore untestable. They are consequently beyond the domain of
4415: Science. This is a common view of some though not all positivists.
4416: 
4417: 2 We don’t study human actions, we study the archaeological record – a
4418: mute collection of stones and bones, matter arranged in time and space
4419: (back to figure 2.1). We can explain what we see in terms of past
4420: cultural systems; their dynamics of change, how they were adapted to
4421: their environment. We do not need to do this with direct reference to
4422: mental factors. Reference to mental factors would involve the error of
4423: mentalist explanation in any case (see chapter 5). Forms of this
4424: argument have been advanced by Binford and others.
4425: 
4426: Many traditional archaeologists also argue that it is difficult if not
4427: impossible to use the archaeological record to recover past beliefs.
4428: Archaeologists like Christopher Hawkes argued for seven levels of
4429: archaeological inference ranging from the relatively straightforward to
4430: the very difficult. Archaeological material, said Hawkes, could be used
4431: with reasonable security to tell us about technology in the past.
4432: Economic inferences were more difficult; inferences about cultural and
4433: religious life were almost impossible except in exceptional
4434: circumstances. This view of the limitations of archaeology was held with
4435: fanaticism by some:
4436: 
4437: I was back in Devizes, along with a packed audience of several hundred
4438: people, to hear the principal living authority on Stonehenge, Professor
4439: Richard Atkinson, give a lecture… When this was done the chairman, the
4440: equally authoritative Professor Stuart Piggott, called for questions.
4441: There came some requests for various elucidations of technical points,
4442: which were given. Then a young man stood up at the back.
4443: 
4444: ‘Would Professor Atkinson care to say anything about the peoples who
4445: built the three main phases – their culture and religion?’
4446: 
4447: He might as well have asked a synod of Methodist ministers whether they
4448: could consider publishing a new edition of De Sade’s 120 Days of Sodom …
4449: All such speculation was futile, the wicked fellow was peremptorily
4450: told. Such information was strictly dependent on written records, the
4451: Stonehenge peoplesdid not have writing, we knew nothing, we would never
4452: know anything; to speculate on such matters was not only totally
4453: unscientific, but far worse, it encouraged the lunatic fringe. In the
4454: slightly shocked silence that followed this icy douche … Professor
4455: Piggott rose to announce that the lecture was at an end. No more
4456: questions. (Fowles 1980: 9–10)
4457: 
4458: Whatever orientation one takes, there are clearly immense difficulties
4459: in recovering thoughts. Behavioural psychologists argue that we can
4460: never ‘know’ what someone else is thinking in the present; we can only
4461: record their behaviour, which can be externally measured and observed.
4462: How much more difficult to recover the minds of women and men not just
4463: long dead, but members of an extinct culture, with an utterly different
4464: view of the world! Such a task is difficult enough for archaeologists of
4465: historic periods; prehistorians have to do so using material remains,
4466: with no documentary records to assist.
4467: 
4468: Why, then, should we be trying to get at past thoughts and beliefs? In
4469: my view, to argue about whether or not it is difficult to do so is to
4470: miss the point. It is simply necessary. I suggest there are three
4471: reasons for this.
4472: 
4473: (1) We all actually make assumptions about past thoughts whether we like
4474: it or not. Take, for example, a dry, straightforward typology of pots.
4475: When archaeologists classify decorations of a certain type together,
4476: they generally do so on the assumption that shared design must have
4477: something to do with shared meanings in the minds of the makers and the
4478: users of the pots, for example ideas of zeitgeist mentioned in Chapter
4479: 5.
4480: 
4481: What a lot of ‘atheoretical’ archaeologists do in practice is assert
4482: that we can never recover past thoughts, but do so in practice by
4483: slipping assumptions about mental attitudes back into their arguments as
4484: ‘common sense’. Thus, for example, putting fireplaces and chimney-stacks
4485: in houses rather than rely on open hearths is common sense because it
4486: makes the house warmer, less full of smoke, more ‘comfortable’. The
4487: problem with such arguments is that they depend on assumptions about
4488: what is ‘natural’ or ‘normal’ in human beings: in this example, a desire
4489: for domestic comfort is assumed as ‘natural’. It is ‘common sense’,
4490: then, to satisfy those desires. These assumptions tend to fall apart
4491: when closely examined, since one can always point to the diversity of
4492: human practice; in particular, different ethnographic examples where the
4493: practice is not ‘n ormal’ or ‘natural’.
4494: 
4495: The problem with common sense in this context is that what is ‘common
4496: sense’ to ‘us’ may not have been common sense to ‘them’. Anthropologists
4497: study other cultures in the present that have very different cultural
4498: attitudes. For the Azande, when some unfortunate accident occurs, it is
4499: common sense to go to a witch-doctor or diviner to find out who was
4500: responsible for the magic behind it. It is logical to assume that other
4501: cultures in the past may have had other common senses.
4502: 
4503: Lurking in the wings behind the common-sense argument are implicit
4504: beliefs in essentialism and ethnocentrism. Essentialism is the belief
4505: that there are certain attitudes or emotions (such as the desire for
4506: privacy, for domestic comfort, or the sex drive) that are ‘natural’ or
4507: biologically endowed, either to humans in general or to a specific sex.
4508: Thus the statement ‘in prehistory, men must have been more warlike than
4509: women as they lacked the mothering instinct’ is an essentialist
4510: statement since it assumes that ‘the mothering instinct’ is endowed
4511: naturally or biologically to all women. Now there may or may not be
4512: certain ‘human universals’ of this kind; that is a matter for debate; I
4513: am sceptical about most of the possible candidates. Whatever the case,
4514: however, such essentialist statements need to be argued through rather
4515: than simply assumed.
4516: 
4517: Ethnocentrism is the belief that the values and attitudes of one’s own
4518: culture are normal and universal. Thus an assumption that, for example,
4519: witchcraft belief is ‘irrational’ is ethnocentric, since it assumes that
4520: Western logic is the only form of rationality around. Belief in
4521: witchcraft must therefore be irrational since it fails to satisfy the
4522: criteria of Western logic. (Of course, Western logic fails to satisfy
4523: the criteria of Azande witch doctors.) Again, belief in the human desire
4524: for privacy is ethnocentric, since it assumes that the high stress on
4525: the individual and hence his or her right to privacy in Western society
4526: is universally normal and natural.
4527: 
4528: (2) Any archaeologist working outside the deeper recesses of prehistory
4529: (that is, before ethnohistorically attested, protoliterate or literate
4530: societies) has to deal with evidence that is strictly ‘historical’ in
4531: nature; that is, with documentary or textual evidence in some form. But
4532: such documents are nothing if not statements of thoughts – however
4533: mundane or ‘obvious’ those thoughts are. If we are therefore going to
4534: relate archaeological to documentary evidence, then, some critical
4535: attention must be given to the mental attitudes and ideas that played
4536: their part in producing that evidence.
4537: 
4538: (3) There is a philosophical point to do with the way we study human
4539: societies. As we explored in the last chapter, it is almost impossible
4540: to describe human behaviour without referring to mental concepts.
4541: Imagine trying to describe – to get a third person to ‘understand’ – the
4542: actions of a woman withdrawing money from a bank solely with reference
4543: to physical movements and actions. This is a point made most elegantly
4544: by one of my favourite and most entertaining writers, the sociologist
4545: Erving Goffman:
4546: 
4547: Take … an act which is tolerably clear: a man driving through a red
4548: light. What is he doing? [Goffman lists 24 different reasons,
4549: including] 1. Where he comes from they have signs, not lights. 2. The
4550: daylight was bad and he couldn’t see. 3. He’s lately become
4551: colour-blind. 4. He was late for work. 5. His wife is giving birth in
4552: the back seat and he’d like to get to a hospital. 6. The bank robber in
4553: the front seat is holding a gun on him and has told him to run the light
4554: … 15. He’s an inspector testing the vigilance of the cops on duty … 22.
4555: He was drunk, high. 23. His mother has a lamentable occupation, and he
4556: has a psychiatrically certifiable compulsivity with regard to red lights
4557: …’ [He concludes]: So our man has passed through a red light. But at his
4558: hearing when the judge asks him what he was doing running a red light,
4559: he will provide an argument as to what was really happening. Obviously,
4560: what makes driving through a red light a discernible, isolable event is
4561: that a rule stipulated in regard to the light was broken. The objective
4562: ‘fact’, then, must be as variable as is the individual’s possible
4563: relation to the rule. (Goffman 1971: 132; italics mine. Note in passing
4564: that we understand the action by reference to its context, a point
4565: developed in the next chapter.)
4566: 
4567: The belief that thoughts and ideas are more important than the material
4568: world is called idealism. Historically, the idealist tradition of
4569: philosophy is a long one, including the names of such philosophers as
4570: Plato, Vico, Berkeley, Kant, Descartes and Hegel, through to the
4571: linguist Ferdinand de Saussure and anthropologist Claude Lévi-Strauss.
4572: Paradoxically, although it stresses the importance of thoughts and
4573: concepts, a belief in idealism can lead to a very conservative
4574: theoretical position. Because thoughts in the idealist view are very
4575: important but also very difficult to get at, all the archaeologist can
4576: legitimately do is describe and classify:
4577: 
4578: Do you wish to understand the true history of a Neolithic Ligurian or
4579: Sicilian? Try, if you can, to become a Neolithic Ligurian or Sicilian in
4580: your mind. If you cannot do that, or do not care to, content yourself
4581: with describing and arranging in series the skulls, implements, and
4582: drawings which have been found belonging to these peoples. (Benedotto
4583: Croce, 1917, Theory and History of Historiography, trans. R.G.
4584: Collingwood 1921: 135)
4585: 
4586: The idealist philosophical tradition has been profoundly influential in
4587: continental Europe, particularly on traditions of French and Italian
4588: archaeology. Postprocessual archaeology was not claimed to be an
4589: idealist philosophy by its advocates, but it was profoundly influenced
4590: by idealist notions.
4591: 
4592: The rest of this chapter will examine three bodies of thought that
4593: archaeologists have used to approach the question of thoughts:
4594: structuralism, Marxism, and ‘cognitive’ approaches.
4595: 
4596: Structuralism
4597: 
4598: One of the key ways in which idealism has affected archaeological
4599: thought is via the school of thought termed structuralism. Like both
4600: functionalism and Marxism, this theoretical current has developed and
4601: changed as a critical tradition over the years. It has few adherents in
4602: its original, classical form today, but (like systems theory) has had
4603: such a profound influence on archaeological thought that it cannot be
4604: ignored. Like Marxism also, structuralism is a term much misused and
4605: abused, referring more to a vague impression of pretentious Paris
4606: intellectuals sitting in cafes on the Rive Gauche than a given set of
4607: ideas. I am going to go back to the roots of structuralism so that its
4608: present influence can be more easily understood.
4609: 
4610: For functionalists, culture is like an organism, different parts of the
4611: body/society performing different functions and the whole adapted to its
4612: environment. For structuralists, culture is like language.
4613: 
4614: Structuralism was originally a body of thought developed in linguistics
4615: by Ferdinand de Saussure. The most important point for our purposes is
4616: to observe that for Saussure, language is composed of hidden rules that
4617: we use but don’t articulate. As I write and you read this sentence, I
4618: use a set of grammatical rules to compose my text. You know that set as
4619: well. Your knowledge of that set of rules enables you to decode, to
4620: ‘read’ what I write. You can do this even though each sentence that I
4621: produce is unique; each sentence is one that has never been composed
4622: before in the history of the English language. Indeed, I can generate a
4623: theoretically infinite set of different statements or sentences from a
4624: fairly simple set of grammatical rules.
4625: 
4626: Both of us, however, understands those rules at a deep, implicit rather
4627: than surface, explicit level. We both have a pretty hazy idea of what a
4628: gerund or a subjunctive clause is. Neither of us puts those rules ‘into
4629: words’ though we ‘know’ and use them constantly and habitually, and in
4630: general we both get them right. (Otherwise, if we did not get them
4631: right, we would not be able to decode each other’s utterances and
4632: understand each other.)
4633: 
4634: In short, the rules that govern language are hidden, deep inside the
4635: human brain. If you want to explain the different forms of language,
4636: then, you need to refer to the hidden (cognitive) rules that generate
4637: sentences.
4638: 
4639: Archaeologists influenced by structuralism suggest that the same is true
4640: of the material things that we find in the archaeological record. They
4641: see artefacts as another expression of human culture in general. If you
4642: want to explain human culture, then, you need to uncover the hidden
4643: (cognitive) rules that generate cultural forms.
4644: 
4645: Structural models have been used to produce classifications of different
4646: kinds of archaeological material. Henry Glassie, for example, used the
4647: analogy of ‘grammar’ to examine the layout of ordinary houses in
4648: eighteenthcentury Virginia. He proposed that ordinary people designed
4649: these houses by taking a series of basic spatial units and applying a
4650: system of grammatical rules to those units to produce different house
4651: plans. He called this scheme a transformational grammar: that is, it
4652: described the way in which units were transformed through a series of
4653: cognitive stages into houses. Similar exercises have been performed for
4654: other classes of material, for example Leroi-Gourhan’s work on
4655: Palaeolithic cave art and schemes of pottery decoration.
4656: 
4657: But the implications of structuralism are deeper than simply offering
4658: tools for fresh classifications. So whereas a processual thinker’s
4659: immediate instinct might be to ask ‘How does this social practice
4660: function within the culture as a whole? And how does this help the
4661: cultural system to work, to be better adapted, within a wider
4662: environment?’, the structuralist will ask ‘What are the underlying rules
4663: governing this structure? And what do those rules tell us about the way
4664: this culture sees the world?’ For functionalists, culture is
4665: fundamentally adaptive, a coping device; for structuralists, culture is
4666: fundamentally expressive, a system of (hidden, cognitive) meanings.
4667: 
4668: Marxism
4669: 
4670: It is impossible at the present time to write history without using a
4671: whole range of concepts directly or indirectly linked to Marx’s thought
4672: and situating oneself within a horizon of thought which has been defined
4673: and described by Marx. One might even wonder what difference there could
4674: ultimately be between being an historian and being a Marxist. (Foucault
4675: 1980: 53)
4676: 
4677: A second school of thought that has had a profound effect on
4678: archaeological thought is that of Marxism. Like structuralism, Marxism
4679: has developed almost beyond recognition since its original formulation
4680: and development by Karl Marx in the nineteenth century. I want to
4681: highlight a very few aspects of Marxist thought that are of relevance to
4682: archaeological theory.
4683: 
4684: In its original form, Marxism is a materialist philosophy. That is, it
4685: proposes that material things are more important than ideas. If so, then
4686: human history is about the growth of human productive power, the growing
4687: human ability as history unfolds to produce material things: Gerry Cohen
4688: suggests that ‘history is, fundamentally, the growth of human productive
4689: power, and forms of society rise and fall accordingly as they enable or
4690: impede that growth’ (Cohen 1978: x). In Karl Marx’s own phrase, humans
4691: are what they do rather than what they think: ‘it is not the
4692: consciousness of men that determines their being, but on the contrary,
4693: their social being that determines their consciousness’ (cited in
4694: McClellan 1977: 389).
4695: 
4696: At any time, Marxists argue, people produce things in a certain
4697: distinctive way, which Marx calls the mode of production. Marxists have
4698: spoken of ‘tribal’, Asiatic, Ancient, feudal and capitalist modes of
4699: production among others. The ancient mode of production, to take one
4700: example, is distinctive for Marx because it relies on slave labour at
4701: its base, whereas feudalism depends on the labour of unfree peasants or
4702: ‘serfs’ tied to the land. Each different mode of production results in a
4703: different kind of class antagonism: in ancient societies between slave
4704: and master, in feudalism between peasant and feudal landlord, in
4705: capitalism between proletarian and bourgeois.
4706: 
4707: The mode of production, Marx argues, can be split into the forces of
4708: production, that is the raw materials, apparatus, labour power, and the
4709: social relations of production. For example, the forces of production in
4710: capitalist society consist of the machines and hardware of the
4711: factories, while the relations comprise the factory system – the
4712: division into workforce and management.
4713: 
4714: For Marx, there will always be antagonism and conflict between these two
4715: elements: ‘the forces of production, the state of society, and
4716: consciousness, can and must come into contradiction with one another,
4717: because the division of labour implies the … fact that intellectual and
4718: moral activity – enjoyment and labour, production and consumption –
4719: devolve on different individuals, and the only possibility of their not
4720: coming into contradiction lies in the negation in its turn of the
4721: division of labour’. For this and other reasons, there will always be
4722: conflict at the heart of human societies. Contradictions and class
4723: antagonisms will tear at the heart of any social formation, developing
4724: slowly or quickly in different circumstances. Eventually, however, they
4725: will bring the whole structure crashing down, and a new social formation
4726: will rise on its ruins.
4727: 
4728: This ‘classical’ model has been much criticized; the road from classical
4729: to modern Marxism is too long to pursue here. I do want to pick out two
4730: key points that emerge from this account of classical Marxism, however,
4731: that have direct relevance to the development of archaeological thought:
4732: 
4733: (1) Marx’s account, if it works, provides a scientific basis to
4734: communism, or more loosely, in this later time, a scientific basis to
4735: political assertions about a more just society. In this sense classical
4736: Marxism shares many parallels with the belief in a positivistic basis to
4737: Science that was discussed in chapter 3. Contrary to positivism,
4738: however, Marx argues forcefully that intellectuals cannot divorce
4739: academic thought from political action.
4740: 
4741: Archaeologists influenced by Marxism, then, have seen a close link
4742: between archaeology and politics. They have seen archaeological practice
4743: and interpretations as partly or wholly political in nature, and in turn
4744: have often seen their own archaeological work as in part a wider
