4745: political exercise. And they argue that to try to deny this is to bury
4746: one’s head in the sand.
4747: 
4748: (2) The process of historical change in the Marxist model is
4749: dialectical. A dialectical model is one that depends on the development
4750: of contradictions and conflict within an entire social formation or
4751: totality. These contradictions can only be understood within that total
4752: social formation. For example, the terms ‘peasant’ and ‘landlord’ do not
4753: have the same meaning regardless of time and place. We can only define
4754: and understand peasant and landlord classes within a total understanding
4755: of the feudal social formation. When that formation breaks down, it
4756: leads to a completely new formation and the development in their turn of
4757: new conflicts.
4758: 
4759: A dialectical model of social process contrasts with other models of
4760: change. In culture history, and earlier forms of systems thinking,
4761: change is often presented as a smooth process of accretion and
4762: adaptation. In place of this, a dialectical model stresses contradiction
4763: and conflict – it was partly as a result of the influence of Marxist
4764: concepts that systems thinking came to be modified as we traced in the
4765: last chapter. The dialectical model also leads us to question social
4766: categories and definitions that claim to be true for all times and all
4767: places.
4768: 
4769: Marxism has thus led archaeologists to question either/or oppositions
4770: such as subjectivity and objectivity. They point out that such terms
4771: only oppose each other within a total framework, and it is that total
4772: framework that they seek to question and transform.
4773: 
4774: As a result, Marxism has had a wide and pervasive influence on different
4775: archaeological traditions around the world. ‘Latin American social
4776: archaeology’ uses a Marxist analytical framework to understand social
4777: formations, and insists on an engagement with social justice issues. It
4778: will be considered in chapter 9.
4779: 
4780: In the old Soviet Russia and other areas of eastern Europe, Marxism was
4781: an official orthodoxy until the break-up of the old Stalinist regimes at
4782: the end of the 1980s. In many cases, the influence of Marxism was
4783: shallow, being confined to ritualized acknowledgement in a Preface or
4784: introduction before a more orthodox descriptive or culture-historical
4785: approach being taken in the text. Ironically, since the fall of
4786: Communism in eastern Europe, there has been more fruitful and sustained
4787: examples of analyses informed by Marxist thought from these countries.
4788: 
4789: Marxism has also been a profound influence on Mediterranean archaeology,
4790: though its influence is not always explicitly laid out in the
4791: literature. For example, there is an ongoing debate over the origins of
4792: Rome and its transformation from a small protohistoric village into an
4793: Imperial city and the hub of the Roman Empire. A major figure in the
4794: archaeology of Roman Italy has been Andrea Carandini, who developed an
4795: interpretation of the rise of Rome that was directly influenced by
4796: Marxist ideas. Subsequent debate over this interpretation was, in part,
4797: an implicitly theoretical debate over the utility of these ideas and
4798: their alternatives.
4799: 
4800: Ideology
4801: 
4802: Later Marxists (or ‘neo-Marxists’), particularly those from different
4803: traditions in western Europe, have focused attention on the role of
4804: ideology in this model. For Marx, the forces and relations of production
4805: were the ‘infrastructure’, the heart of the system: the political and
4806: legal system sat on top of this, along with a set of ideological
4807: beliefs. In a simple view, while social foundations are creaking and
4808: society becomes increasingly unequal and unjust, people’s beliefs act to
4809: ‘paper over the cracks’, to make the existing system appear legitimate.
4810: 
4811: ‘Vulgar’ ideology is obvious: in our society, one might cite
4812: flag-waving, advertising, appeals to patriotism, motherhood and apple
4813: pie. But Marxists believe ideology also works on a far more subtle level
4814: than this. In particular, ideology:
4815: 
4816: 1 naturalizes; that is, it makes the existing social order with all its
4817: inequalities appear timeless, God-given, ‘just scientific fact’, or
4818: without any conceivable alternative;
4819: 
4820: 2 makes interests that are sectional (for example, the economic
4821: wellbeing of the upper classes) appear universal (of benefit to
4822: everyone); and
4823: 
4824: 3 masks what is ‘really going on’, for example by denying that social or
4825: economic inequalities exist.
4826: 
4827: To take a straightforward (and therefore overly simplistic, ‘vulgar’ and
4828: easily decoded) example, a Marxist would argue that we only have to open
4829: the pages of most newspapers to see ideology at work (‘competition and
4830: the rules of the free market are not arbitrary human constructions that
4831: can be changed, they are out there in nature; look at how cold, wet,
4832: miserable life was in the Middle Ages, in other words, before the
4833: benefits of capitalism’). Sectional interests are presented as universal
4834: (wage rises are presented as ‘harming the nation’ rather than just
4835: harming the profits accruing to the wealthy capitalists). An ideology of
4836: equality (‘anyone can become President, everyone is equal under the law,
4837: I want to build a classless society’) masks what Marxists see as very
4838: real divisions and inequalities in gender and wealth.
4839: 
4840: This interest in ideology has led to the detailed exploration of how
4841: ideology works, and a stress on the unmasking of the relations behind
4842: ideology. Paradoxically, then, Marxism started as a materialist model,
4843: but has ended up influencing Anglo-American archaeology through its
4844: analysis of ideological beliefs and practices. This move away from
4845: materialism is one of the key points behind the Frankfurt School of
4846: critical theory. The Frankfurt School, most famously in the work of
4847: Jürgen Habermas, sought to look behind the mask of ideology, to show how
4848: modern Western systems of belief were not neutral or objective, but
4849: actually ideological constructions that legitimated modern capitalism.
4850: It aimed to emancipate ordinary people and promote democractic values by
4851: heightening consciousness of the power and pervasiveness of ideology.
4852: 
4853: Of course, interest in ideology in archaeology has two aspects. One can
4854: look at ideology in the past, how for example a particular belief system
4855: legitimated the position of an élite in ancient societies. But one can
4856: also look at the present – how archaeological writing is itself
4857: ideological. For example, Bruce Trigger has written extensively on how
4858: different interpretations of North American prehistory served to
4859: ideologically portray Native American cultures as backward and
4860: unchanging.
4861: 
4862: Cognitive Archaeology
4863: 
4864: A third body of thought, developed largely by archaeologists themselves,
4865: has emerged in the last 20 years: that of ‘cognitive archaeology’.
4866: Cognitive archaeology aims to ‘develop a secure methodology by which we
4867: can hope to learn how the minds of the ancient communities in question
4868: worked and the manner in which that working shaped their actions’
4869: (Renfrew 2005: 41). Cognitive archaeologists often describe themselves
4870: as working within the general assumptions and framework of
4871: processualism. Consequently, much of this work describes itself as
4872: cognitive-processualism by its advocates.
4873: 
4874: These authors look at ‘mind’ in two ways. First, cognitive
4875: archaeologists are interested in human origins, and specifically the
4876: role of cognitive development in the evolutionary origins and
4877: development of hominids. For example, archaeologists have asked about
4878: possible correlates in the archaeological record for the development of
4879: language, such as the development of more complex chaines operatoires in
4880: the production of stone tools. This work has close links with the
4881: application of Darwinian models to archaeology, in that it deals with a
4882: process that is at least in part about the biological development of
4883: species, and uses insights and observations from evolutionary psychology
4884: (see chapter 10).
4885: 
4886: The second interest is in cognitive processes in human societies after
4887: the emergence of our own species, Homo sapiens sapiens. Renfrew and
4888: Zubrow have argued that, for example, we can identify religious or cult
4889: behaviour in the archaeological record. They have suggested that there
4890: is no contradiction between a broadly scientific view of archaeological
4891: theory and an insistence that we need to look at cognition. Kent
4892: Flannery and Joyce Marcus, for example, have looked at functional
4893: linkages between the ‘ideological subsystem’ and other areas of cultural
4894: subsystems, and stressed the compatibility of their work with
4895: subsistence and settlement analysis:
4896: 
4897: Our own first effort [in cognitive archaeology: Flannery and Marcus
4898: 1976] was an attempt to understand the ancient Zapotec Indians more
4899: fully by combining their cosmological beliefs with a more traditional
4900: analysis of their subsistence and settlement. … We simply tried to show
4901: that one could explain a higher proportion of ancient Zapotec
4902: subsistence behaviour if, instead of restricting oneself to a study of
4903: agricultural plants and irrigation canals, one took into account what
4904: was known of Zapotec notions about the relationships of lightning, rain,
4905: blood sacrifice, and the ‘satisfizing ethic’. We also stressed that we
4906: could only do so because the 16th-century Spanish eyewitness accounts of
4907: the Zapotec were so rich. (Flannery and Marcus 1993: 260)
4908: 
4909: Flannery and Marcus identify the study of cosmology, religion, ideology
4910: and iconography as legitimate areas of cognitive analysis, stressing
4911: that all such work must be empirically grounded. Again, Steven Mithen
4912: has talked of ‘thoughtful foragers’ – looking at how we might model the
4913: decision-making processes of hunters and gatherers within an adaptive
4914: framework, yielding a range of cognitive inferences (Mithen 1990: see
4915: also chapter 10, this volume).
4916: 
4917: The argument of all these authors is that we can look at thoughts while
4918: retaining the essential components of a processual approach: belief in
4919: scientific objectivity and ‘testing’. But other archaeologists have
4920: claimed that the need to look at thoughts has led them to question the
4921: very basis of processual archaeology, as we will see in the next
4922: chapter.
4923: 
4924: All this is very interesting, but we’ve heard very little in this
4925: chapter about archaeology. How, precisely, do the different views lead
4926: to concretely different archaeological explanations?
4927: 
4928: I’ll be discussing some more practical examples in the next chapter, but
4929: here is an ‘interim summary’ for the time being.
4930: 
4931: For many in the processual tradition, the artefacts we find are evidence
4932: of different parts of a cultural system that existed in the past. For
4933: some, artefacts form a fossil record of human behaviour. Many
4934: processualists argue that as we cannot see thoughts scientifically, so
4935: we can’t explain artefacts in terms of the ideas in the minds of the
4936: makers – though following Flannery and Marcus, we can include ‘cognitive
4937: variables’ in our analyses, if used with caution. Instead, we look at
4938: different parts of past cultural systems and the links between them.
4939: Processualists claim to have the ability to test between alternative
4940: hypotheses about the way those systems worked through the development of
4941: middle-range theory.
4942: 
4943: For structuralists, artefacts are evidence of systems of belief in the
4944: broadest sense, evidence of ‘mind-sets’ or ‘worldviews’. Just as
4945: language is structured by rules that remain hidden, so is material
4946: culture. Structuralists look at oppositions in the particular form of
4947: artefacts, the placing of artefacts within graves, for ideas about the
4948: constitution of gender, oppositions between nature and culture, and so
4949: on. Since we can never prove the existence of hidden rules, this
4950: tradition suggests that we can never really ‘test’ any single
4951: interpretation of the past; there is a tendency towards developing
4952: multiple, complex interpretations rather than trying to reduce culture
4953: to one single pattern.
4954: 
4955: Marxists look for contradictions and inequalities in culture. For
4956: example, is burial practice legitimating or masking what happens in
4957: life? Marxists ask: how does the belief or worldview expressed by this
4958: piece of material culture relate to what is really going on? For
4959: example, does an egalitarian burial practice mask inequalities in
4960: society?
4961: 
4962: Conclusion
4963: 
4964: In the second part of this chapter, I have picked out developments
4965: within three schools of thought within social theory as a whole –
4966: Marxism, structuralism and cognitive archaeology. I could have looked at
4967: other profoundly influential movements treated elsewhere in this book
4968: (feminism, the reaction against unilinear social evolution). These three
4969: movements, however, give some flavour of the intellectual currents that
4970: came to influence archaeological theory in the 1980s.
4971: 
4972: Marxism gives us an interest in conflict and contradiction, a stress on
4973: ideology, an insistence that academic discourse is fundamentally also
4974: political discourse in the present, and a model of structure and agency.
4975: Structuralism gives us an interest in the hidden workings of language,
4976: an interest in cultural meanings and in culture as expressive. Cognitive
4977: archaeology, though fully developed as a body of theory more recently
4978: than the 1980s, originated in part as an insistence that looking at
4979: thoughts is not just an admissible scholarly activity, but a central
4980: part of the archaeological endeavour.
4981: 
4982: All these themes were taken up into a group of new theoretical strands
4983: in the 1980s, that came to be known collectively as ‘postprocessual
4984: archaeology’.
4985: 
4986: 7
4987:
